questions.questionText: How many lines of symmetry does a square have?
questions.explanation: A square has four lines of symmetry: two connecting midpoints of opposite sides, and two connecting opposite vertices (diagonals).

questions.questionText: How many lines of symmetry does a rectangle (that is not a square) have?
questions.explanation: A rectangle (not a square) has two lines of symmetry, which pass through the midpoints of opposite sides.

questions.questionText: How many lines of symmetry does an equilateral triangle have?
questions.explanation: An equilateral triangle has three lines of symmetry, each passing through a vertex and the midpoint of the opposite side.

questions.questionText: How many lines of symmetry does an isosceles triangle (that is not equilateral) have?
questions.explanation: An isosceles triangle has one line of symmetry, which passes through the vertex between the two equal sides and the midpoint of the base.

questions.questionText: How many lines of symmetry does a regular pentagon have?
questions.explanation: A regular pentagon has five lines of symmetry, each passing through a vertex and the midpoint of the opposite side.

questions.questionText: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a square?
questions.explanation: A square looks identical after rotations of 90°, 180°, 270°, and 360°. So, it has rotational symmetry of order 4.

questions.questionText: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a rectangle (that is not a square)?
questions.explanation: A rectangle (not a square) looks identical after rotations of 180° and 360°. So, it has rotational symmetry of order 2.

questions.questionText: What is the order of rotational symmetry of an equilateral triangle?
questions.explanation: An equilateral triangle looks identical after rotations of 120°, 240°, and 360°. So, it has rotational symmetry of order 3.

questions.questionText: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a regular hexagon?
questions.explanation: A regular hexagon looks identical after rotations of 60°, 120°, 180°, 240°, 300°, and 360°. So, it has rotational symmetry of order 6.

questions.questionText: Which of these shapes has exactly one line of symmetry?
questions.explanation: An isosceles trapezium has one line of symmetry, which bisects the parallel sides. A square has 4, a rectangle has 2, and a parallelogram has 0 lines of symmetry.

questions.questionText: Which of these shapes has no lines of symmetry but has rotational symmetry of order 2?
questions.explanation: A parallelogram has no lines of symmetry but has rotational symmetry of order 2 (it looks the same after a 180° rotation). A rhombus has 2 lines of symmetry, a kite has 1, and a regular pentagon has 5 lines and order 5 rotational symmetry.

questions.questionText: Which capital letter has both at least one line of symmetry and rotational symmetry of order 2?
questions.explanation: The letter 'H' has two lines of symmetry (horizontal and vertical) and rotational symmetry of order 2. 'A' has one line of symmetry but no rotational symmetry of order 2. 'F' has no symmetry. 'Z' has rotational symmetry of order 2 but no lines of symmetry.
